metadata.title: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = transmitted wave / cleared step; CRIMSON = reflected wave / partial bounce
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no probability-current bookkeeping, no R/T amplitude algebra, no evanescent-case detour.

beats.narration_text: Roll a ball off a downhill step and it always keeps going. Send a quantum particle at the same step with more than enough energy to clear it — and part of the wave bounces back.

card.copy: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear

beats.narration_text: Classical ball, downhill step: the ball speeds up on the lower side. Energy is conserved, the ball keeps rolling. Reflection is zero — there is nothing to push it back.
A quantum particle with twice the energy it needs to clear the step still reflects — not always, but sometimes. How can a wave reflect off a step it has plenty of energy to pass?

card.copy: Enough energy to clear the step. Classical: always passes. Quantum: partly bounces. What is doing the reflecting?

beats.narration_text: On the left side the particle has one kinetic energy — one wavelength. On the right side, after the step, it has more kinetic energy — a shorter, faster wavelength.

The wave function must match at the boundary: same value and same slope on both sides. The only way to satisfy both conditions with two different wavelengths is to split — some wave reflects and some transmits.

Think of it as an impedance mismatch — the same mechanism that reflects sound at a wall or light at a glass surface. When a wave crosses into a medium with a different wave speed, some of it bounces at the interface.

The reflection coefficient depends on how abruptly the wavelength changes. A gradual slope reflects almost nothing; a sharp step reflects the most. And remarkably, a step down reflects just as well as a step up.
If the step height is V-zero and the particle has energy E, the reflection goes as the ratio of the wave numbers: R equals k-one minus k-two, all squared, divided by k-one plus k-two, all squared.

Illustrative example: an electron with energy 4 eV hits a step down of 3 eV, so the far side has 7 eV of kinetic energy. The wave numbers differ by about 30 percent and roughly 2 percent of the wave reflects — completely classically forbidden.
It is not the energy that causes reflection. It is the mismatch in wavelength at the boundary. Wherever the wavelength changes suddenly, a quantum wave splits — part passes, part bounces back.

card.copy: Not the energy — the mismatch. Any sudden wavelength change splits the wave. Part passes. Part bounces.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World series.
